constructs.name: Exit theme
constructs.definition: The primary grievance or reason-for-leaving a respondent foregrounds in their exit response. One label per unit: the theme the respondent treats as decisive, not every theme mentioned.

include: Label the theme the respondent treats as the deciding factor, even when several are mentioned.
Sarcasm counts for its intended meaning, not its surface words.
Comp-process complaints (frozen cycles, band compression, capped accelerators) are pay.
exclude: Do not label themes mentioned only to be dismissed (“the pay was fine” is not pay).
General praise or goodbye messages with no grievance are other.
Do not infer a theme from metadata; the text alone decides.

edgeCases: Mixed pay + growth (“underpaid and unpromotable”): choose the one framed as decisive; if truly equal, prefer the first stated.
RTO framed as commute burden is remote-policy, not workload.
A beloved manager leaving (absence) is management, even with no criticism of any person.

examples.text: Base salary sat 18% under market for my level and the refresh grants never came.
examples.label: pay

examples.text: The pay was fine, honestly — it was watching every good manager leave while the broken processes stayed.
examples.label: management

examples.text: Pay was fair, manager was kind, I just moved cities for family.
examples.label: other

examples.text: Oh sure, the workload was totally reasonable. Eighty-hour weeks build character, right?
examples.label: workload-burnout

constructs.name: Burnout severity
constructs.definition: How much sustained exhaustion or depletion the respondent expresses about their own state — not workload volume in the abstract, but its toll on the person.

include: Health consequences (doctor visits, sleep, family impact) read as severe.
Sarcastic minimization of extreme hours is at least mild.
exclude: Complaints about workload fairness without personal toll are none.
Burnout attributed to others (‘my team is burned out’) does not score the respondent.

edgeCases: ‘The pace was not survivable’ — severe: survivability language is somatic even without symptoms.

examples.text: On-call every third week for a year. I did the math at my doctor's office.
examples.label: severe

examples.text: Loved the people. Left for money.
examples.label: none

importProposals.name: Voluntary vs regretted exit
importProposals.definition: From the legacy 2019 codebook: whether the organization would re-hire the respondent into the same role (regretted) or not (non-regretted).

include: High performers and hard-to-fill roles read regretted.

importProposals.name: Exit tone
importProposals.definition: From the legacy codebook: overall tone of the exit response from hostile to warm.

include: Score the close, not the complaint.

draftProposals.name: Preventable exit
draftProposals.definition: The respondent describes a departure the organization could plausibly have prevented — a named, fixable grievance (comp, manager, workload, path) rather than relocation, family, or pure opportunity.

include: A specific fixable cause is named as decisive (comp process, staffing, promised promotion).
exclude: Moves framed as personal (family, relocation, career change) with no organizational grievance.

edgeCases: “Left for money” with no complaint about the comp process → not preventable unless a raise was requested and refused.

examples.text: I gave finance two comp cycles to fix it. They didn't, so a competitor did.
examples.label: present

examples.text: Pay was fair, manager was kind, I just moved cities for family.
examples.label: absent

draftProposals.name: Counteroffer mentioned
draftProposals.definition: The response mentions a counteroffer — received, refused, arriving too late, or conspicuously absent — anywhere in the exit account.

include: Any reference to a retention offer, match, or its absence counts, however brief.
exclude: Generic comp complaints with no offer narrative are absent.

edgeCases: “They suddenly found budget when I resigned” → present: that is a counteroffer story without the word.

examples.text: Loved the people. Left for money. It really is that simple.
examples.label: absent

themes.name: Counteroffer timing
themes.definition: Hypothesis from the inductive pass: the respondent mentions a counteroffer arriving too late or not at all — concentrated in Sales.

themes.name: Manager absence
themes.definition: The decisive grievance is a missing or churned-through manager rather than a bad one.

inductiveTaxonomy.note: Hypothesis generation, not measurement — themes arrive exploratory and Director-glyphed.
